seo.title: Pattani Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Pattani Province travel guide: Kru Se Mosque (historic 16th-century ruins), Pattani Central Mosque (largest in Thailand), Gulf coastline, and deep-south Malay-Muslim culture. Area: 1,940 km², 12 districts. Plan your visit.

overview: Pattani is one of Thailand's most historically significant and culturally distinct provinces, tracing its identity to the Malay sultanate of Patani — a major trading power that once controlled much of the northern Malay peninsula. Today the province is home to a Malay-Muslim majority whose language, dress, food, and religious practice differ markedly from central Thailand. Pattani's skyline is punctuated by mosque domes rather than Buddhist chedis, and the Jawi script (Arabic-based Malay) appears alongside Thai on signboards throughout the province. The Gulf of Thailand coastline offers quiet beaches and a traditional fishing industry, while the interior is given to rubber and palm oil plantations. Pattani is also the site of Thailand's most historically important mosques: the atmospheric ruins of Kru Se Mosque (16th century) and the magnificent Pattani Central Mosque, the largest mosque in Thailand. Travelers should note that Pattani is part of Thailand's deep-south conflict zone; see the travel advisory section below for current guidance.
geography: Pattani Province occupies 1,940 km² on the Gulf of Thailand coast at the base of the Thai-Malay peninsula. The terrain is predominantly flat coastal lowland rising to low forested hills in the south and west toward the border with Yala. The Pattani River (Mae Nam Pattani) flows northward through the province to empty into the Gulf near Pattani city, forming a broad estuary that historically anchored the sultanate's maritime trade. The coastline stretches roughly 70 km and is fringed by fishing villages, mangrove stands, and beaches that see almost no international tourism. Inland, the landscape is dominated by rubber tree plantations, paddyfields, and palm oil groves. The province borders Narathiwat to the east, Yala to the south, and Songkhla to the west.
highlights: Kru Se Mosque (Masjid Krue Se) — atmospheric 16th-century mosque ruins, never completed according to legend, sacred to local Muslims and the most historically significant Islamic site in Thailand
Pattani Central Mosque (Masjid Klang Pattani) — the largest mosque in Thailand, a striking turquoise-domed structure completed in 1963 accommodating up to 15,000 worshippers
Pattani Old Town & Waterfront — Chinese-Malay shophouse architecture lining the Pattani River estuary; the old port quarter retains painted clan houses and Peranakan-influenced buildings
Lim Ko Niaw Shrine — a Chinese goddess shrine sacred to both the local Chinese community and Malay Muslims, marking the legend of a Chinese princess who came to Pattani
Hat Ruso & Hat Talo Kapo — quiet Gulf of Thailand beaches south of Pattani city, popular with local families; largely undeveloped and offering an authentic glimpse of southern coastal life

Pattani Fishing Villages — traditional stilted sea villages along the Gulf coast; the province is one of Thailand's most productive fishing regions, with fresh seafood markets at Pattani pier every morning
Yarang Ancient Town — archaeological site of the pre-Islamic Malay Hindu-Buddhist kingdom of Langkasuka, with excavated foundations and artifacts predating the sultanate era
Wat Chang Hai Ratburanaram — one of the most revered Buddhist temples in the deep south, enshrining the body of the monk Luang Pho Thuat, attracting Buddhist pilgrims from across Thailand

travelAdvisory.summary: Pattani is part of Thailand's deep-south conflict zone (together with Narathiwat, Yala, and parts of Songkhla), where a separatist insurgency has been ongoing since 2004. Sporadic bombings, arson attacks, and targeted shootings occur, primarily directed at military and government targets but with civilian casualties. The Thai government maintains a significant security presence, including military checkpoints on major roads. Most western governments — including the UK, US, Australia, and EU member states — advise against all but essential travel to Pattani, Narathiwat, and Yala provinces.
travelAdvisory.practicalGuidance: International leisure tourists rarely visit Pattani. Those who do for journalistic, academic, or professional purposes should register with their embassy, avoid traveling after dark, stay in well-reviewed hotels with security staff, and follow the advice of local contacts. Checkpoints are routine — carry your passport at all times. The situation fluctuates; check your government's current travel advisory immediately before any trip. The conflict has no reliable seasonal pattern.

sources.sourceName: UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office — Thailand Travel Advice
US State Department — Thailand Travel Advisory

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Jan–Apr: The driest and sunniest months on Pattani's Gulf coast. Temperatures hover between 26–34°C with low humidity and reliable sunshine. The fishing season is at its most active and seafood markets are fully stocked.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: May–Jun and Oct–Nov: Transitional months with patchy rain. The province is rarely busy with visitors at any time of year, so 'shoulder season' distinctions are less meaningful here than in tourist-heavy provinces.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jul–Sep and Nov–Dec: The Gulf of Thailand monsoon brings heavy rainfall, rough seas, and occasional flooding in low-lying coastal areas. The Pattani River can flood in November. Fishing activity reduces and outdoor access is limited.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit January to April for the best weather. The dry season allows comfortable outdoor exploration of Kru Se Mosque, the old town, and coastal areas. Be aware that all travel to Pattani requires consulting current security advisories regardless of season.

topAttractions.name: Kru Se Mosque (Masjid Krue Se)
topAttractions.description: The most historically important Islamic monument in Thailand — a 16th-century brick mosque whose construction was, according to local legend, never completed. The story holds that a Chinese princess cursed the mosque, preventing its roof from ever being finished. The roofless structure stands today as a protected historical site and a pilgrimage destination for Malay Muslims. The brickwork, arched doorways, and surviving mihrab (prayer niche) show a fusion of early Islamic and Hindu-Malay architectural styles. Located 7 km east of Pattani city center on the bank of the Krue Se stream. Entry is free but respectful dress (head covering for women) is expected.

topAttractions.name: Pattani Central Mosque (Masjid Klang Pattani)
topAttractions.description: The largest mosque in Thailand — a striking structure completed in 1963 featuring four soaring white minarets and a signature turquoise dome visible from across Pattani city. The mosque can accommodate up to 15,000 worshippers during Friday prayers and the two Eid celebrations, when the surrounding streets fill with devotees in traditional Malay dress. Non-Muslim visitors are welcome outside of prayer times; dress conservatively (long trousers, covered arms, head covering for women) and remove shoes before entering. The interior features intricate geometric tilework, Arabic calligraphy, and a grand prayer hall.

topAttractions.name: Lim Ko Niaw Shrine (San Chao Leng Chu Kiang)
topAttractions.description: A revered Chinese shrine on the Pattani riverfront honoring Lim Ko Niaw, a Chinese princess said to have come to Pattani in the 17th century in search of her brother. The legend intertwines with the story of Kru Se Mosque — Lim Ko Niaw is credited with the curse that halted its construction. The shrine is sacred to Pattani's Chinese community and draws Buddhist worshippers as well as local Malay Muslims who regard the princess as a holy figure. The annual Lim Ko Niaw Festival (around March–April) features a colorful street procession and firewalking ceremony.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Thailand — Lim Ko Niaw Shrine

topAttractions.name: Yarang Ancient Town (Langkasuka)
topAttractions.description: An archaeological zone approximately 15 km south of Pattani city preserving the remains of the ancient Hindu-Buddhist Malay kingdom of Langkasuka, which flourished from roughly the 2nd to the 14th century CE before Islam arrived on the peninsula. Excavations have uncovered brick foundation platforms (makam), ceramics, and terracotta artifacts. The site is modest by the standards of Ayutthaya or Sukhothai but has significant academic importance as evidence of the pre-Islamic cultural layer beneath Pattani's Islamic identity. A small local museum at the site displays excavated finds.

topAttractions.name: Wat Chang Hai Ratburanaram
topAttractions.description: One of the most revered Buddhist temples in the deep south, located approximately 14 km west of Pattani city in Khok Pho district. The temple enshrines the preserved body of the highly venerated monk Luang Pho Thuat (1582–1682), whose image appears on protective amulets worn by millions of Thais nationwide. The temple receives a steady stream of Buddhist pilgrims from across Thailand and serves as a meaningful counterpoint to Pattani's predominantly Islamic cityscape. The main viharn housing the monk's body is surrounded by ornate pavilions and a sacred pond.

topAttractions.name: Pattani Old Town & Riverside Waterfront
topAttractions.description: The historic commercial core of Pattani city along the Pattani River estuary, where centuries of maritime trade left behind a distinctive blend of Chinese shophouse and Peranakan architecture. Colorful clan houses, Chinese temples, and old warehouses line the riverside lanes. The waterfront is most atmospheric at dawn when fishing boats unload their catch at the morning market (talad pla). Streets like Yarang Road and Pipit Road retain original painted facades and ornate doorways from the early 20th century. The old town is compact enough to explore on foot in two to three hours.

localFood: Khao Yam — the quintessential deep-south breakfast: rice mixed with toasted coconut, dried shrimp, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves, bean sprouts, and pomelo, dressed with budu (fermented fish sauce); Pattani's version is widely regarded as one of the best in the south

Budu — pungent fermented anchovy sauce that is both a condiment and a cooking base unique to the Thai deep south and northern Malaysia; used to dress vegetables, rice, and grilled fish at nearly every local meal

Roti Kaeng — flaky Malay flatbread (roti canai) served with rich southern Thai curry, most often gaeng massaman or gaeng leung (sour yellow curry); a standard breakfast and mid-morning snack at Muslim street stalls
Nasi Kerabu — Malay-style blue rice (colored with butterfly pea flowers) served with grilled chicken, coconut sambal, pickled vegetables, and fried shallots; a visually distinctive Pattani specialty

Satay Madura — grilled skewers of chicken or beef in a Malay-Javanese style marinade, served with compressed rice cakes (ketupat) and peanut sauce; sold at street stalls throughout Pattani city in the evenings
Kanom Jeen Nam Ya — fresh rice noodles served with a thick, aromatic fish-based curry sauce, topped with fresh herbs and raw vegetables; a midday staple at local Muslim eateries

Ikan Bakar (Grilled Fish) — whole fish marinated in turmeric, galangal, and lemongrass then grilled over charcoal; the Gulf of Thailand catch includes red snapper, mackerel, and barramundi available fresh at the Pattani pier market

Khanom Buang Yuan (Vietnamese-style crispy crepes) — a sweet snack sold by street vendors near the old town; thin crispy shells filled with sweetened coconut cream and shredded coconut, a culinary legacy of Vietnamese traders who settled in Pattani centuries ago
culture: Pattani's cultural identity is rooted in the Malay sultanate of Patani, which at its height in the 16th and 17th centuries was one of the most powerful and cosmopolitan trading states on the Malay peninsula — attracting Arab, Persian, Chinese, Portuguese, and Dutch merchants. The arrival of Islam in the 15th century transformed the region, and today over 80% of Pattani's population are Malay Muslims who speak Pattani Malay (locally called Bahasa Jawi or Yawi) as their first language. The Jawi script is used in religious education and on mosque signage, and traditional Islamic scholarship remains central to community life through the pondok (religious boarding school) system. Despite nearly a century of formal Thai administration, Pattani retains a distinct cultural personality. Festivals follow the Islamic calendar — Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha are the major celebrations — alongside local traditions like the Lim Ko Niaw Festival (which uniquely blends Chinese and Malay religious elements). Traditional Malay performing arts including wayang kulit (shadow puppetry), dikir barat (a call-and-response group singing tradition), and silat (Malay martial art) survive in villages and cultural centers.
gettingThere: Pattani does not have a commercial airport; the nearest is Hat Yai International Airport (HDY) in Songkhla Province, approximately 110 km west (roughly 1.5–2 hours by road). From Hat Yai Airport, shared minivans and buses depart for Pattani from the Hat Yai bus terminal (~120 THB, 2 hours). Direct air-conditioned buses connect Pattani to Bangkok's Southern Bus Terminal (about 12–14 hours, from ~500 THB) and to Hat Yai, Yala, and Narathiwat. Pattani city has a small bus terminal on the western edge of town. There is no passenger train service to Pattani — the southern rail line passes through Yala, not Pattani. Travelers driving from Hat Yai should use Route 43 east; military checkpoints on the approach roads are routine and require passport presentation.
gettingAround: Pattani city is compact and can largely be explored on foot or by motorcycle taxi (10–30 THB for short hops within the city). Songthaews (shared pickup trucks) run fixed routes between Pattani city and outlying districts for 20–40 THB. There is no Grab ride-hailing coverage in Pattani — private taxis can be arranged through hotels or at the bus terminal for roughly 300–500 THB for half-day hire. Renting a motorcycle (from ~250 THB/day) from guesthouses or local rental shops is the most flexible option for visiting Kru Se Mosque (7 km east), Wat Chang Hai (14 km west), and coastal areas. Driving after dark is strongly discouraged given the security situation; all road trips should be completed before sunset.
travelTips: Check your government's current travel advisory before any visit — Pattani, Yala, and Narathiwat are subject to ongoing security advisories from most western governments
Carry your passport at all times; military and police checkpoints on major roads are routine and you will be asked to show identification
Dress conservatively throughout the province — women should cover arms and legs, and a head covering is required when entering the Central Mosque or Kru Se Mosque
Kru Se Mosque is open to respectful visitors outside of the five daily prayer times; avoid visiting on Fridays when prayers attract large congregations
Pattani's food scene is entirely halal — pork and alcohol are almost entirely absent from restaurants and markets; this is one of the best provinces in Thailand for Malay-Muslim cuisine
The morning fish market at Pattani pier (around 05:00–08:00) is one of the most atmospheric seafood markets in the south — arrive early for the best action
Lim Ko Niaw Festival (held in spring, roughly February–April depending on the lunar calendar) is the most spectacular annual event in Pattani and worth planning around if security conditions permit
Avoid photographing military installations, checkpoints, or soldiers — it can cause unnecessary complications; ask permission before photographing mosques and shrines
